I mean I try I mostly try and as you said just Ignore it and you know mush on but I Don't think you can beat it other than situationally First being nice and then in the cases where you really have a stubborn person who now knows You don't want them to do X and Those they shouldn't be doing X and they perpetuate in doing X now if they're in your group You got to throw them out if they're a subscriber You got to cut them off and you may have to formally cease and desist and you know go through all of that stuff But to my knowledge and nobody has a good answer for the question that you've just asked We all deal with it literally on a day-to-day person-to-person piece-to-piece sort of episodic incidental basis What you said if If you said you're never gonna have a good answer for it I guess the answer is to ignore it because if you're always gonna be unhappy Better ignore it and be you can't totally ignore it right right because the really egregious stuff Has to be stopped right And by the way, it's why I always advise people to Don't get involved in Trademarking and patenting and all of that any more than you must Your few key pieces of intellectual property. Yes, but otherwise you become the Bill McGowan line About MCI you become a phone call, you know a law firm with an antenna on the roof You know and not so companies like Disney they have to right and they got as many lawyers as they got elves and rabbits and You know and they got to go after like everybody because otherwise they lose the right to protect and And and that's not you know a fun way to do business or go through life But we have you know We have an incident a guy in a foreign country for example last year Who's a good guy and he's been with us for a long time and he should know better You know you like shake your head and you say surely this guy knew better and maybe he did maybe he's operating under the easier To apologize than it is to get permission theory.
